course_description: Theory and practice of economic policy. Fiscal & monetary policy in open economy. Fixed versus flexible exchange rates. Economic interdependence and international business cycles. Growth and development policy. Money and inflation in Australia. Political business cycles, central bank independence and inflation targeting. Fiscal policy and inflation. Labour markets and unemployment.

requirements.assumed_background: It is assumed that students will have previously covered material in the area of Macroeconomics at the introductory level and also some study at the intermediate level e.g. a sound knowledge of the closed economy ISLM model, open economy ISLM model, AD-AS modeling framework and the Neoclassical growth model.

timetable:
Timetables are available on mySI-net.

Additional Timetable Information Tutorials for this course begin in Teaching Week 2. Students should sign up for tutorials using mySi-net. Sign-on will be available from 10:30am Wednesday 14 July until 12:00pm Thursday 12 August .

Next 2. Aims
course_aims:
The aim of this course is to further provide students with an understanding of the key relationships and causal links between well-defined major macroeconomic aggregates. This develops an understanding of the role and workings of economic policy and provides a coherent, analytical framework from which to critically evaluate these policies. The exposition of economic policy in this course is quite general in nature, although effort is made to focus the course material selectively on some of the major economic issues important in the contemporary Australian and global context.

The global financial crisis has called into question the theory and policy perspectives of an influential segment of the discipline whose models are based on efficient markets. Critiques from within and from (slightly) outside the mainstream will be included in this course. Also discussed are the practical problems faced by policy-makers when they are confronted with unfamiliar problems. As employers of macroeconomists value a graduate's ability to understand and even anticipate policy responses (especially changes in interest rates), we will try to get inside the heads of policy makers. This involves understanding the models that organise their thinking and looking at the facts that affect their practical judgements.

learning_outcomes.description: Command and utilise a core, comprehensive and well-founded knowledge of the field of modern macroeconomics, in particular, international linkages and influences on the Australian macroeconomy.
Better understand applied macroeconomic issues, and knowledge and understanding of economic data.
Have the ability to define and analyse problems based on modeling frameworks and an understanding of relevant data and institutional detail.
Identify key contemporary macroeconomic problems, both domestic and international and discuss possible solutions and potential innovations towards improving current policies and practices.
Collect, analyse, and organise information and ideas independently through wider reading and to convey those ideas clearly and fluently.
Obtain analytical skills and capacity for lateral thinking.
Understand the theory and its assumptions so that better policy recommendations can be made

assessment_details.title: ECON2040 Mid Semester Examination
assessment_details.category: Exam - Mid Semester Outside Scheduled Class
assessment_details.task_description:
The test is on a Saturday. If the test can be scheduled for week 7 (Sept. 11), the intention is that the 90 minute exam will cover the first 5 lectures, 4 tutorials and other associated assessable reading materials.

You will be advised of the date (and therefore the exact coverage) as soon as the University Administration can finish their scheduling and let me or Christina know. Past papers will be of some significant, though partial, use, but note that the course structure and content has changed in 2010 as a different lecturer is conducting the course.

Learn.UQ

or
mySI-net

to access marking criteria held in this profile.

assessment_details.title: ECON2040 Final Exam
assessment_details.format: Multiple-choice, Short answer, Short essay, Problem solving
assessment_details.task_description:
The final examination is designed to cover all learning objectives and to test both depth and breadth of students knowledge relevant to the course. (The end of semester examination will be two hours in duration, plus the usual 10 minutes perusal, and will consist of material dealt with from ALL of the lectures and tutorials throughout the semester, with additional emphasis on the material covered after the midsemester examination.)

There will be an essay (or a series of short essays) set on a pre-announced topics relating to policy-setting in the Australian context, with MCQs and/or short-answer questions. Some questions focus specifically on the extra readings designed to widen your knowledge. the rest of exam focuses mostly on analytical material (graphical or mathematical problem-solving). If the mid-semester test indicates areas of weakness, these may be assessed again on the final exam to encourage the class to respond to assessment feedback provided after the test.

learning_resources.other_learning_resources:
Note that much assessable material is based on materials beyond the textbook; e.g., what is discussed in the lectures, tutorials and in extra specified readings etc. (If something is not assessable, this should be made clear. If in doubt, ask me, the lecturer.)

The textbook was also used in ECON2020, Macroeconomic Theory. You have therefore already studied much of it closely. This means that other material beyond the textbook will play a significant role in the study (and assessment) of ECON2040.

Useful supplements to this textbook (including some practice materials relevant to assessment) are available on the internet at: www.mhhe.com/au/dornbusch2e . You are strongly encouraged to examine this supplement to confirm your knowledge of the textbook. To obtain the username and password to access the student section of these online supplementary materials, you will need a registration code, which is included on the card at the front of the textbook. If you do not want or need to purchase the book, but still wish to access the online content, then you are able to purchase an access code either through the bookstore, or by contacting McGraw-Hill customer service (phone 1800 646 572, or cservice_sydney@mcgraw-hill.com ).

The overheads, tutorial questions and answer guides, additional recommended readings and practice materials will be available on the ECON2040 website on Blackboard in reasonably timely fashion. Extra, more detailed, supplementary materials, will be posted after some lectures. (There may be a lag because some will be written after the relevant lecture or in response to macroeconomic events.) Certainly you need to take your own detailed lecture notes during lectures and to read the required text and other required reading materials.

Previous 2. Aims

Next 4. Activities

learning_activities.topic: Information (Lecture Series): This is the revised Lecture Schedule: Bruce Littleboy, weeks 1-4; Phil Bodman, week 5; John Quiggin week 6 onwards. John Quiggin will provide materials and readings on Blackboard (BB). You will be advised of any sections from the textbook that provide supporting material. Note: the date of the Saturday test is not yet known, and there is no lecture on the Tuesday before the test. Estimates of time for each topic may be inaccurate because there are new lectures and lecturers. If you miss a lecture, check where we are up to. Reminders: required/assessable reading can include material posted or listed on BB during the semester. Tutorials contain assessable material and they start in week 2.

Topic 1: Orientation (Lecture): Challenges to theory and policy posed by the GFC. The standard goals, methods and rationale. (Is the economy like a machine?) The two-way relationship between micro and macro processes. Types of shocks and cycles (check BB); the variety of macro-dynamics Readings/Ref: Blackboard (Supplementary materials posted after the lecture.); DBCFS2e (Parts of Ch. 13 and Ch. 17 - see BB.)

Topic 2: Traditional Theories of Policy Making (Lecture): Demand shocks. Rules vs discretion (intro); stop-go; lags. Instruments and targets (Tinbergen); assignment of instruments to targets; trade-offs. The political business cycle (how positive analysis differs from normative analysis) Readings/Ref: Blackboard (Supp notes (BB), extra reading and tutorial); DBCFS2e (Ch. 15.3-4; 19.1-8; 19.11)

Topic 3: Deciding Monetary Policy (Lecture): Forecasting via leading indicators. The macro-economy and the stance of monetary policy; inflation targeting and its IS-LM depiction; deflation; how central bankers think. Readings/Ref: Blackboard (Supp notes and tutorial); DBCFS2e (Ch. 15, esp. 15.5-15.6, 15.1-15.3 reviews ECON1020; Ch. 16.1 (The yield curve), Ch. 17.4, 17.9.)

Topic 4: Policy and Economic Growth (Lecture): [PB] Neoclassical versus endogenous growth; economic convergence debate; two-sector models and poverty traps; population growth; public and social infrastructure; natural resources, the environment and recycling; the Truly Poor countries institutions, aid, trade and FDI. Readings/Ref: DBCFS2e (ch. 4, revise ch. 3 re the neoclassical model)

Topic 5a: Postwar Keynesianism (Lecture): [JQ] Post-war Keynesianism, Phillips curve, fiscal vs monetary policy debate, inflation expectations and dynamics, Friedman, rational expectations, NCE. Readings/Ref: DBCFS2e (Chs 11.7, 16, Insight 13.5, ch. 13.3)

No lecture on Tuesday: Saturday Midsemester Test (Exam): DATE AND COVERAGE SUBJECT TO CHANGE To permit preparation for the test, there is no lecture on the Tuesday prior to the test. (If the test is on the 11th, there is no lecture on the 7th.) On material from weeks 1-5 inclusive, if the test is held on week 7 or 8. See Blackboard for details of coverage and exact date, time and venue(s).

Topic 5b: Theories of Policy-making before the GFC (Lecture): Inflation and monetary policy; New Classical & RBC models, New Keynesians, convergence Lucas critique and stabilization Taylor rules and central bank independence: with a focus on the Australian experience.
Topic 6: Financial globalization & the GFC (Lecture): The Open Economy and the development of the Global Financial Crisis

Topic 7: Responding to Crises (Lecture): The GFC (incorporating Open Economy issues).Causes and consequences of crises; Theories about market-driven crises (esp. Minsky, Leijonhufvud) Globalisation, current accounts and international business cycles Inefficient asset markets and financial markets; voluntary vs involuntary unemployment Policies during crises; lessons learned? Recent restoration of the stabilisation role of fiscal policy (and "functional finance")

policies_and_procedures:
University Policies & Guidelines

An overview of the Universitys assessment-related policies can be found on myAdvisor ( http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2910 ).

Academic Integrity
It is the University's task to encourage ethical scholarship and to inform students and staff about the institutional standards of academic behaviour expected of them in learning, teaching and research. Students have a responsibility to maintain the highest standards of academic integrity in their work. Students must not cheat in examinations or other forms of assessment and must ensure they do not plagiarise.

Plagiarism
The University has adopted the following definition of plagiarism:

Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

Students are encouraged to read the UQ Academic Integrity and Plagiarism policy ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25128 ) which makes a comprehensive statement about the University's approach to plagiarism, including the approved use of plagiarism detection software, the consequences of plagiarism and the principles associated with preventing plagiarism.

Feedback on Assessment
Feedback is essential to effective learning and students can expect to receive appropriate and timely feedback on all assessment. For a detailed explanation of the feedback you are entitled to, you should consult the policy on Student Access to Feedback on Assessment. ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25109 )

As a student you have a responsibility to incorporate feedback into your learning; make use of the assessment criteria that you are given; be aware of the rules, policies and other documents related to assessment; and provide teachers with feedback on their assessment practices.

There are certain steps you can take if you feel your result does not reflect your performance. Please refer to the myAdvisor web site. ( http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2953 )

School of Economics
Assessment Guidelines

Definition of Plagiarism

Examples of plagiarism as given in the Universitys Academic Integrity and Plagiarism Policy are:

Direct copying of paragraphs, sentences, a single sentence or significant parts of a sentence;

Direct copying of paragraphs, sentences, a single sentence or significant parts of a sentence with an end reference but without quotation marks around the copied text;

Copying ideas, concepts, research results, computer codes, statistical tables, designs, images, sounds or text or any combination of these;

Paraphrasing, summarising or simply rearranging another person's words, ideas, etc without changing the basic structure and/or meaning of the text;

Offering an idea or interpretation that is not one's own without identifying whose idea or interpretation it is;

A cut and paste' of statements from multiple sources;

Presenting as independent, work done in collaboration with others;

Copying or adapting another student's original work into a submitted assessment item.

Importance of Referencing

Complete and accurate referencing is essential for academic writing. There are three main reasons why full referencing is essential.

The first is that when another writers work is used without being referenced the act of plagiarism has been committed. Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as ones own work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another and is covered by the Universitys Academic Integrity and Plagiarism Policy . The penalty for plagiarism under the Student Discipline and Misconduct Statute can range from a fine not exceeding 1 penalty point to exclusion from the University.

The second reason for referencing is so that anyone reading the paper can follow up any ideas or concepts that have been presented. This is especially important in academic writing where people involved in research may read the paper. One of the main ways of researching a topic is to read what other people have written and then follow up some of the references they have cited. Then, after those follow-up papers have been read the researcher can continue the search-trail by tracking down more references cited in those follow-up papers, and so on. This is known as a bibliographical chain. This research is difficult if not impossible to complete if papers are not fully and accurately referenced.

Thirdly, referencing is important as it adds credibility to the argument that you are presenting. If an argument is to have any credibility it must be supported by evidence and that evidence must be referenced.

If a reader of your paper can read something that you have written and ask questions like Who says? or How do you know that? or On what do you base that comment? then what you have written needs to be referenced. For more information on the importance of, and direction on, referencing please refer to the School of Economics Assignment Presentation Guide .

Consequences of Plagiarism

Students should be aware that the School of Economics does not hesitate to use the procedures available under the University of Queensland Statute No. 4 (Student Discipline and Misconduct) 1999 to investigate suspected misconduct involving plagiarism.

The penalties imposed for cases where a finding of misconduct is made vary, based on the seriousness of the case and range across but are not limited to:

a fine not exceeding 1 penalty unit;

a reduction in the mark for the assessment piece or cancellation of the mark for the assessment piece;

a requirement to complete further or repeat work for the subject;

the cancellation of credit for the subject;

expulsion from the University.

Students should also be aware of the fact that when a finding of misconduct is made that this is recorded on their student record.

University Policies and Guidelines

Placement Courses
Students on a placement course also known as a work placement, internship, industry study, industry experience, clinical practice, clinical placement, practical work, practicum, fieldwork, teaching practice should refer to the University policy, Placement Courses ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25120 ) for detailed information.

Working with Children
Students whose studies include a professional/work placement, internship, clinical practice, teaching practice or other similar activity which involves them in regular contact with children should refer to the University policy, Working with Children Check - "blue card" ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25004 ) to find out how to apply for a blue card.

Students with a Disability
Any student with a disability who may require alternative academic arrangements, including assessment, in the course/program is encouraged to seek advice at the commencement of the semester from a Disability Adviser at Student Support Services. Refer to the University policy, Students with a Disability (Disability Action Plan) ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25122 ) and to the policy on Special Arrangements for Examinations for Students with a Disability ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25111

While it is the responsibility of the relevant faculty to liaise with professional and registration bodies regarding the acceptability of any adjustment to an academic program, the University Health Service can arrange appropriate advice and assistance on personal and public health issues.

Occupational Health and Safety
Undergraduate Students ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25055 ) and Postgraduate Students ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25057 ) should be familiar with the University policies on occupational health and safety in the laboratory.

Previous 5. Assessment

Next Summary
course_introduction:
This course covers topics in modern macroeconomic policy at the intermediate level and builds significantly on the material covered in the required prerequisite ECON2020 Macroeconomic Theory course at this level. The core aim of this course is to provide further understanding of the key relationships and causal links between well-defined major macroeconomic aggregates. This provides a coherent, analytical framework within which to evaluate these policies. The exposition of economic policy in this course is often quite general ("theoretical") in nature, although effort is made to focus the course material selectively on some of the major economic issues important in the contemporary Australian and global context. We will try to see how central banks and fiscal authorities understand how economies work and how policies take effect. A recurrent theme in this course is the recent revival of Keynesian-style intervention and the associated re-assessments of Keynes's economic theories.

Emphasis will be placed on economic policymaking and the use and understanding of coherent and consistent frameworks in which to analyse the workings of the macroeconomy and on which to build and critique economic policy. Time permitting, coverage will include: the efficient use of policy instruments to strive to achieve the targets of economic policy, time consistency of policy, microfoundations of modern macroeconomics, trend economic growth and policy, issues in macroeconomic stabilisation and policy effectiveness both under "normal" and under crisis conditions, the rational expectations hypothesis (its importance and its limitations), voluntary and involuntary unemployment and the peculiarities of the labour market, the resurgence of fiscal policy, tax reform, constraints imposed by budget deficits and public debt (the meaning of "sound finance"), and political and partisan cycles that affect how government policy in practice may depart from those policies most economists may recommend. There are many theories that explain the business cycle, and you will be introduced to some of them.

The trend in macroeconomics is towards understanding aggregate economic activity using models with well-defined, consistent foundations at the microeconomic level. There is a fundamental interconnection between macroeconomics and microeconomics. So some elementary concepts from microeconomics will be used in this course. Disagreements about macroeconomics often reflect disagreements about how to model the choices individuals make. A beautiful model does not always help us predict events in a messy world.

Simple algebra and elementary calculus will sometimes be used in the development and explanation of economic frameworks. Expect to see economic analysis explained graphically, mathematically and in words. An understanding of economic models is a means of comprehending the role and probable effectiveness of economic policy. Parts of the assessment will be devoted specifically to the mathematical aspects of the course.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

This course if offered by Dr Bruce Littleboy and Professor John Quiggin for the first time, so student feedback is not available at this time. The course overlaps with the one offered last year and uses the same textbook, but more directly address issues of theory and policy raised by the GFC. We have preserved the flexible weightings of assessment. We look forward to receiving your responses at the end of the semester.

graduate_attributes.intro: Successfully completing this course will contribute to the recognition of your attainment of the following UQ (Undergrad Pass) graduate attributes:

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A1 . A comprehensive and well-founded knowledge in the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A4 . An understanding of how other disciplines relate to the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A5 . An international perspective on the field of study.
B1 . The ability to collect , analyse and organise information and ideas and to convey those ideas clearly and fluently , in both written and spoken forms .
B2 . The ability to interact effectively with others in order to work towards a common outcome.
B3 . The ability to select and use the appropriate level , style and means of communication .
B4 . The ability to engage effectively and appropriately with information and communication technologies .
C1 . The ability to work and learn independently .
C3 . The ability to generate ideas and adapt innovatively to changing environments.
C4 . The ability to identify problems , create solutions , innovate and improve current practices .
D1 . The ability to define and analyse problems .
D2 . The ability to apply critical reasoning to issues through independent thought and informed judgement .
D3 . The ability to evaluate opinions , make decisions and to reflect critically on the justifications for decisions.
E1 . An understanding of social and civic responsibility .
E2 . An appreciation of the philosophical and social contexts of a discipline.
E4 . A knowledge and respect of ethics and ethical standards in relation to a major area of study.
E5 . A knowledge of other cultures and times and an appreciation of cultural diversity .

other_learning_activities_information:
You are strongly encouraged to attend all of the teaching sessions. The lectures and tutorials have been specifically designed to aid your learning of the course material. Failure to attend either a lecture or a tutorial session may result in you being seriously disadvantaged. In particular, tutorial attendance is strongly recommended as an important supplement to lecture attendance. In the tutorials you will be able to clarify important points that you have not yet grasped. You will also practice the model solving and model manipulation skills you will need to do the mid-semester and final exams.

Note that tutorial attendance will be recorded to be used in making the final allocation of grades for those students whose formal assessment leaves them at the margin of obtaining a higher grade.

Previous 3. Resources

Next 5. Assessment
course_grading:
Grade X: No assessable work received.

Grade 1, Fail: Fails to demonstrate most or all of the basic requirements of the course:

Complete failure to demonstrate sufficient basic knowledge and understanding of key concepts, relationships and models. Awarded for an overall mark in the range 0-19 percent inclusive.

Grade 2, Fail: Demonstrates clear deficiencies in understanding and applying fundamental concepts; communicates information or ideas in ways that are frequently incomplete or confusing and give little attention to the conventions of the discipline:

Failure to demonstrate sufficient basic knowledge of key concepts, relationships and models. Awarded for an overall mark in the range 20-44 percent inclusive.

The minimum percentage required for a grade of 2 is: 20%

Grade 3, Fail: Demonstrates superficial or partial or faulty understanding of the fundamental concepts of the field of study and limited ability to apply these concepts; presents undeveloped or inappropriate or unsupported arguments; communicates information or ideas with lack of clarity and inconsistent adherence to the conventions of the discipline:

Some basic knowledge is evident, but detail and precision are patently absent at a level sufficient to merit competency and therefore a passing grade. Awarded for an overall mark in the range 45-49 percent inclusive.

The minimum percentage required for a grade of 3 is: 45%

Grade 4, Pass: Demonstrates adequate understanding and application of the fundamental concepts of the field of study; develops routine arguments or decisions and provides acceptable justification; communicates information and ideas adequately in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

A basic knowledge of core concepts and modelling techniques obtained. Awarded for an overall mark in the range 50-64 percent inclusive.

The minimum percentage required for a grade of 4 is: 50%

Grade 5, Credit: Demonstrates substantial understanding of fundamental concepts of the field of study and ability to apply these concepts in a variety of contexts; develops or adapts convincing arguments and provides coherent justification; communicates information and ideas clearly and fluently in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

A more thorough and accurate understanding of modelling and policy analysis displayed through both written and verbal means. Awarded for an overall mark in the range 65-74 percent inclusive.

The minimum percentage required for a grade of 5 is: 65%

Grade 6, Distinction: As for 5, with frequent evidence of originality in defining and analysing issues or problems and in creating solutions; uses a level, style and means of communication appropriate to the discipline and the audience:

A thorough, accurate and comprehensive knowledge of concepts and model analysis displayed and some evidence of wider reading. Awarded for an overall mark in the range 75-84 percent inclusive.

The minimum percentage required for a grade of 6 is: 75%

Grade 7, High Distinction: As for 6, with consistent evidence of substantial originality and insight in identifying, generating and communicating competing arguments, perspectives or problem solving approaches; critically evaluates problems, their solutions and implications:

Accuracy and attention to detail displayed in all analysis together with significant evidence of critical evaluation of material and of wider reading. Requires an overall mark of 85 percent and above.

The minimum percentage required for a grade of 7 is: 85%

Other Requirements & Comments:

Note that both lecture and tutorial attendance will be monitored in this course. Tutorial attendance will be kept. Information concerning their tutorial attendance may be used in making final judgements concerning the allocation of grades for those students whose formal assessment leaves them at the margin of obtaining a higher grade.
other_assessment_information:
The School of Economics is committed to reducing the incidence of plagiarism. Further information on plagiarism and how to avoid an allegation of plagiarism is available under Section 6. Policies and Guidelines - 6.1. Assessment Related Policies & Guidelines of this Course Profile.

Scheme 1: Midsemester Exam 30%, Final Exam 70%
Scheme 2: Midsemester Exam 50%, Final Exam 50%

In this course, there is flexible weighting. For the purpose of determining your final grade and reporting final scores, we will use the scheme that maximises your score and therefore your grade.

Note that tutorial attendance will be monitored. This will be used in helping to make decisions about final grades for students whose formal assessment leaves them at the margin of obtaining a higher grade.

SPECIAL EXAMINATIONS

If you are unable to attend a scheduled exam (e.g. due to illness or other unavoidable circumstances which can be evidenced - e.g. medical certificate), you may be eligible to sit a special examination at a later time. Note that the mid-semester exam (test) is a SCHOOL exam, and the final exam is a CENTRAL EXAM.

Information about special examinations can be found on the web at http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/special-examinations . Please read this information very carefully.

All applications for special exams must be submitted within (5) days of the original date of the examination.

Mid-Semester Examination (SCHOOL exam) - submit your application to the Course Administrator, Christina Ellsworth, with appropriate documentation attached. Do not submit applications to the Faculty Collaborative Learning Centre or the Student Centre.

End of Semester Examination (CENTRAL exam) - submit your application to the Student Centre, Level 1, J.D. Story Building with appropriate documentation. Do not submit applications to the Course Administrator.

SUPPLEMENTARY EXAMINATIONS

If you fail a course (generally with a grade of three), you can find information about supplementary examinations at : http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/supplementary-examinations-2901 . Read this information very carefully to see if you may be eligible to apply for a supplementary examination. Also see General Award Rules, Section 1A.17.3, and your program rules.

Applications for supplementary exams must be lodged online via mySI-net, within 5 days of the official release of results.

Important Information regarding Supplementary Examinations for courses within the Faculty of Business, Economics & Law:

· Supplementary Examinations are weighted at 100% (i.e. progressive assessment does not count) and therefore ALL modules and topics are assessable.

· Results for Supplementary Examinations cannot be graded higher than a four (4).

· Once approved, applications cannot be rescinded.

· Students are advised to take the Special & Supplementary Exam period into account when making post-examination plans (15-17 December).
